query: glycopyrrolate vs atropine promethazine scopolamine preanesthetic

retrieved_docs.title: Antisialagogues -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The injectable forms of glycopyrrolate and atropine bear an FDA-approved indication for use in peri-operative secretion reduction, though neither has explicit approval for the reduction of noisy breathing near death. Scopolamine hydrobromide injection is available for use outside of the United States and is dosed at 0.3 to 0.6 g to reduce secretions.

retrieved_docs.title: Glycopyrrolate -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Other commonly used anticholinergics include atropine and scopolamine. Most frequently, clinicians use glycopyrrolate to reduce pharyngeal, tracheal, bronchial, and sialagogue effects preoperatively; decreased secretions are the desired effect during anesthesia when a tracheal tube is in place. A blockade of reflexive vagal cardiac inhibition reflexes may also occur during intubation and anesthetic induction. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Preoperative Management -- Issues of Concern -- Anticholinergics
retrieved_docs.content: Atropine, glycopyrrolate, and transdermal scopolamine are anticholinergic agents used to decrease airway secretions and to prevent bradycardia during anesthetic induction. The bradycardia can result from a combination of the application of volatile agents such as halothane and nasopharyngeal and airway instrumentation. The use of anticholinergics for bradycardia has been decreasing in recent years. Atropine can be given orally with appropriate timing, while glycopyrrolate is given only via a parenteral route. Side effects include the use of anticholinergics include CNS irritability, temperature elevation, dry mouth, and flushing.

retrieved_docs.title: Antisialagogues -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: A small study performed in thirteen patients demonstrated greater efficacy of glycopyrronium over scopolamine for death rattle. [7] A larger trial showed that hyoscine bromine 0.4 mg was superior to glycopyrrolate 0.2 mg in reducing a noise score (nursing assessment combined with noise meter reading). Glycopyrrolate was further associated with a greater need for repeat injection and added sedation [8] . The dose of glycopyrrolate for secretion reduction usually is 0.4 mcg/kg; this dosing disparity may limit the findings of this study.

retrieved_docs.title: Glycopyrrolate -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Glycopyrrolate is used perioperatively as a muscarinic receptor antagonist. [2] [3] The topical formulation of glycopyrrolate is indicated to treat primary axillary hyperhidrosis in patients 9 and older. [4] Glycopyrrolate is also helpful in reducing severe or chronic drooling in pediatric patients with neurologic conditions, such as cerebral palsy. The intravenous formulation of glycopyrrolate classically reverses vagal reflexes and bradycardia intraoperatively and reverses the muscarinic effects of cholinergic agents such as neostigmine or pyridostigmine. [5] Glycopyrrolate may be administered to reverse the neuromuscular blockade due to nondepolarizing muscle relaxants postoperatively and is frequently used in conjunction with neostigmine, a cholinesterase inhibitor. [6] Various oral inhalation formulations of glycopyrrolate are indicated for the long-term maintenance treatment of airflow obstruction in patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Glycopyrrolate -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Glycopyrrolate is a muscarinic receptor antagonist and an essential anticholinergic agent in perioperative care and various medical settings. Clinicians use this medication to inhibit salivary and respiratory secretions, achieve antisialagogue effects, and prevent reflex bradycardia during surgical procedures. By targeting muscarinic receptors, glycopyrrolate provides critical support in maintaining physiological stability and optimizing conditions for surgical interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: Glycopyrrolate -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Glycopyrrolate, also known as glycopyrronium, is an anticholinergic medication. This drug is a synthetically created quaternary amine with pyridine and a cyclopentane moiety within the compound's structure. Glycopyrrolate has been widely used as a preoperative medication to inhibit salivary gland and respiratory secretions. Anticholinergics are typically administered to produce an antisialagogue, sedative, or amnesic effect and to prevent reflex bradycardia. These drugs are not predictably effective in increasing gastric fluid pH or decreasing gastric fluid volume. Glycopyrrolate is among the most commonly used anticholinergic medications. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Antiemetic Medications -- Administration -- Anticholinergics
retrieved_docs.content: Scopolamine is the most commonly encountered medication in this class. It works by antagonizing the M1 muscarinic receptor. It is predominantly used to treat motion sickness or prophylactically in the perioperative setting. Side effects are typically mild but include dry mouth, vision changes, or drowsiness. The administration is transdermal. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Oculocardiac Reflex -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The occurrence of the reflex may be reduced by choosing the appropriate anesthetic, as some agents are associated with a greater risk of OCR activation than others. Studies indicate that pretreatment with intravenous anticholinergics like atropine or glycopyrrolate decreases OCR incidence. Atropine blocks peripheral cardiac muscarinic receptors, increasing sinoatrial node firing and atrioventricular node conduction and countering OCR's vagal outflow.

retrieved_docs.title: Strategies to Reduce Polypharmacy in Older Adults -- Issues of Concern -- Anticholinergics
retrieved_docs.content: Anticholinergic medications can cause delirium, memory loss, hallucinations, blurry vision, constipation, dry mouth, elevated body temperature, and increased risk of falls. These drugs can also precipitate acute angle-closure glaucoma and urinary retention. First-generation antihistamines, such as chlorpheniramine, hydroxyzine, promethazine, meclizine, and diphenhydramine; antiparkinsonian drugs, such as benztropine and trihexyphenidyl; skeletal muscle relaxants, such as cyclobenzaprine and methocarbamol; antidepressants, such as amitriptyline, desipramine, nortriptyline, and paroxetine; antimuscarinics, such as oxybutynin, solifenacin, trospium, and tolterodine; antipsychotics, such as chlorpromazine, clozapine, olanzapine, and thioridazine; and antispasmodics, such as scopolamine, hyoscyamine, and atropine, are commonly prescribed medications with anticholinergic properties.

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Nausea -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The treatment of PONV is most effective with a combination of agents and prevention is more effective than initiating therapy after the onset of symptoms. Many facilities have developed standardized algorithms based on risk scores from the factors listed above. For instance, having 4 risk factors may correlate to approximately 80% chance of PONV. In these instances, some algorithms would recommend 4 antiemetic agents be used prophylactically for prevention. Agents used as antiemetics include serotonin antagonists (5-HT3 antagonists) such as ondansetron, antidopaminergic agents such as droperidol, antihistamines such as dimenhydrinate, and the anticholinergic transdermal medication scopolamine (best applied preoperatively). Additionally, low doses of the steroid dexamethasone are commonly given as a first or second-line treatment, and new medication aprepitant (NK1 antagonist) has proven particularly useful for prevention of PONV when given in the preoperative period. Metoclopramide, in addition to decreasing aspiration risk through increasing lower esophageal sphincter tone and enhancing gastric emptying, also has antiemetic effects. [3] [10] [11] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Anticholinergic Medications -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Benztropine and trihexyphenidyl: Used to counter reduced dopamine levels and relieve symptoms of Parkinson disease [6] Oxybutynin and tolterodine: FDA approved for the treatment of urge incontinence and detrusor hyperactivity [6] Oxybutynin has an off-label use for the treatment of hyperhidrosis. Diphenhydramine and other anti-histamines: FDA approved as a sleeping aid Scopolamine: Used as a prophylactic anti-emetic [8] Atropine: Used to dilate pupil during retina visualization and in the treatment of cholinergic toxicity [9] Vecuronium and Succinylcholine: Antinicotinic medications used as a neuromuscular blockade in surgeries [6] Mecamylamine: Used strictly in research settings as a ganglionic blocker [6] Glycopyrrolate: A type of quaternary amine used in anesthesia to decrease salivary and tracheal secretions [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Parasympathomimetic Medications -- Indications -- Indirect-Acting Parasympathomimetics
retrieved_docs.content: Donepezil, Rivastigmine, and Galantamine: Alzheimer disease [3] Edrophonium: Historically used in the diagnosis of myasthenia gravis [4] Neostigmine: Postoperative and neurogenic ileus and urinary retention, myasthenia gravis, postoperative reversal of neuromuscular blockade Physostigmine: Antidote for anticholinergic toxicity [5] Pyridostigmine: Myasthenia gravis [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Autonomic Pharmacology -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Scopolamine: This drug prevents motion sickness and postoperative nausea or vomiting. Oxybutynin: This drug addresses urge incontinence and postoperative bladder spasms. Dicyclomine and glycopyrrolate: These drugs are used for abdominal pain associated with irritable bowel syndrome. Glycopyrrolate also aids in cholinesterase reversal of neuromuscular blockades in postoperative anesthesia to prevent bronchospasm and is being investigated as an adjunct treatment in COPD. [10] [11] [12] [13] [14] [15] [16] Adrenoreceptor agonists or sympathomimetics: The drugs belonging to this category are listed below. Clonidine: This drug is an antihypertensive agent. Dobutamine, phenylephrine, and epinephrine: These drugs are used to correct severe hypotension in cardiogenic shock and acute heart failure. Epinephrine is also used in ACLS guidelines for non-shockable heart rhythms in cardiac arrest and for rapid reversal of fatal anaphylactic reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: Muscarinic Antagonists -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Anesthesiologists frequently use muscarinic receptor antagonists to reverse non-depolarizing neuromuscular blocking agents (NMBA) after a surgical procedure. Neostigmine is the most common agent employed for reversing non-depolarizing NMBA and functions by inhibiting acetylcholinesterase, resulting in an increased amount of ACh in the neuromuscular junction. Clinicians can administer the muscarinic receptor antagonist glycopyrrolate to attenuate the muscarinic side effects of the increased ACh from neostigmine administration. Glycopyrrolate minimizes the muscarinic side effects of neostigmine, such as salivation, lacrimation, diarrhea, bradycardia, and bronchoconstriction. Therefore, neostigmine with glycopyrrolate results in overcoming the paralysis of skeletal muscles caused by non-depolarizing NMBA without the manifestation of the cholinergic muscarinic side effects. Moreover, atropine and glycopyrrolate are muscarinic receptor antagonistic agents used by anesthesiologists to induce bronchodilation to manage severe bronchospasm that is refractory to treatment. Such efforts include 100% oxygen with hand ventilation, deepening of the anesthetic via administering a bolus of intravenous (IV) propofol or ketamine, and albuterol administration. [6] [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Atropine -- Indications -- Anti-sialagogue effects
retrieved_docs.content: While atropine can be used independently for anti-salivation effects, it is not formally recommended for routine use in controlled airways. However, it can be used off-label to minimize secretions in the intubated patient. Glycopyrrolate is at least 5 times more potent than atropine in its anti-sialagogue effect.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1555
retrieved_docs.content: Aprepitant, scopolamine (skopolamin) and trimethobenzamide hydrochloride are antiemetic agents that may prevent الغَثَيان (nausea). Each of these medications is employed in clinical practice to reduce or avert nausea, contributing to improved patient comfort and outcomes in settings where nausea is a concern.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1407
retrieved_docs.content: Aprepitant, scopolamine, and trimethobenzamide hydrochloride are agents that may prevent nausea, while hydroxyzine hydrochloride may be used to treat nausea. These medications function as antiemetic options in clinical practice, offering both prophylactic and therapeutic strategies for managing nausea in patients. Their use can be tailored to the underlying cause and severity of nausea, contributing to improved symptom control and patient comfort.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1374
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamin and bethanechol are both classified as parasympathomimetics, a group of agents that mimic the activity of the parasympathetic nervous system. By stimulating cholinergic pathways, these drugs enhance parasympathetic tone, which can be therapeutically useful in conditions where increased muscarinic activity is desired. Their shared classification underscores a common mechanism of action despite being used for different clinical indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2145
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergic beta‑Antagonists [MoA] exert their pharmacologic effect by antagonizing beta‑1 adrenergic receptors, thereby reducing sympathetic stimulation of the heart and other tissues. Because this mechanism opposes the cholinergic activity of methacholin, the use of Adrenergic beta‑Antagonists [MoA] is contraindicated in patients receiving methacholin, as the conflicting actions can lead to adverse physiological effects. Clinicians should therefore avoid combining these agents to ensure safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2786
retrieved_docs.content: Aprepitant, hydroxyzine hydrochloride, and nabilone are pharmacologic agents that can be employed in the management of الغَثَيان. Aprepitant may prevent الغَثَيان, while both hydroxyzine hydrochloride and nabilone may treat الغَثَيان. The use of these medications, each with distinct mechanisms of action, provides clinicians with multiple therapeutic options for preventing or alleviating nausea in patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1927
retrieved_docs.content: Aprepitant, skopolamin, and nabilone are pharmacologic agents that are linked to the management of الغَثَيان (nausea). Aprepitant may prevent الغَثَيان, and skopolamin may also prevent this symptom. In addition, nabilone may be used to treat الغَثَيان. Together, these drugs represent different therapeutic options for preventing or alleviating nausea, highlighting the importance of selecting appropriate antiemetic strategies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1808
retrieved_docs.content: The ATC category “Other antiemetics” comprises several pharmacologic agents that share a common therapeutic purpose of preventing and treating nausea and vomiting. Skopolamin, metopimazine, and casopitant are all classified as members of this “Other antiemetics” group within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system. Their inclusion in the same ATC class reflects similar antiemetic activity, helping clinicians identify and select appropriate medications for managing emesis across various clinical settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2734
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergic beta‑Antagonists, whose mechanism of action involves blocking β‑adrenergic receptors, have a contraindicated mechanism of action with methacholin, a muscarinic receptor agonist. Because the pharmacologic effects of β‑blockade oppose the cholinergic stimulation produced by methacholin—potentially leading to adverse outcomes such as enhanced bronchoconstriction or undesirable cardiovascular effects—these agents should not be used together in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 0 -- Community 13
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the community’s knowledge delineates how anticholinergic and adrenergic agents are organized by selectivity, route of administration, and combination therapy, and it clarifies their specific indications and contraindications in acute inflammatory respiratory conditions such as acute bronchitis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2907
retrieved_docs.content: Aprepitant, skopolamin, and hydroxyzine hydrochloride are pharmacologic agents that can be used in the management of nausea. Both aprepitant and skopolamin may prevent the onset of nausea, whereas hydroxyzine hydrochloride may be employed to treat nausea once it is present. Appropriately selecting among these agents can help control nausea and improve patient comfort.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 60
retrieved_docs.content: with methacholin, a bronchoconstrictor and miotic agent. Methacholin belongs to the miotic class, which also includes aceclidine; miotics and sympatholytics are related pharmacologic groups that can influence ocular physiology. Overall, the network of beta‑blocking agents, their receptor targets, ocular applications as antiglaucoma drugs, interactions with anxiolytics, contraindications with specific anti‑arrhythmics and bronchoconstrictor/miotic agents, and their placement within the sympatholytic and beta‑1 adrenergic antagonist hierarchies provides a comprehensive framework for safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2937
retrieved_docs.content: Skopolamin may be used to prevent الغَثَيان (nausea), while both nabilone and diphenhydramine hydrochloride may be employed to treat الغَثَيان. These agents represent pharmacologic options for the prevention and management of nausea in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1504
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamin and edrofonium are both classified as parasympathomimetic agents, meaning they mimic the activity of the parasympathetic nervous system. As members of this pharmacologic class, they enhance cholinergic transmission and can be used to support functions mediated by acetylcholine, such as cognition, memory, and certain autonomic processes. Recognizing both galantamin and edrofonium as parasympathomimetics helps clinicians understand their mechanism of action and potential therapeutic applications in conditions where augmentation of parasympathetic activity is desired.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2827
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamine and ambenonium chloride are both classified as parasympathomimetic agents, meaning they mimic the activity of the parasympathetic nervous system by stimulating cholinergic receptors and enhancing parasympathetic tone. These drugs are used in clinical contexts where increased cholinergic activity is beneficial, such as in the treatment of certain neurological or autonomic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1955
retrieved_docs.content: Guaithylline is identified as a bronchodilator, meaning it promotes relaxation of airway smooth muscle and can be used to alleviate bronchoconstriction. In contrast, atropine is not classified as a bronchodilator; its pharmacological profile is inversely related to bronchodilation, reflecting an anticholinergic mechanism that influences airway tone differently. Recognizing that Guaithylline directly induces bronchodilation while atropine’s effects are opposite in nature assists clinicians in selecting appropriate agents for managing respiratory conditions involving airway constriction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1554
retrieved_docs.content: Other antiemetics in ATC is an Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical classification that includes the antiemetic agents metopimazine, nabilone, and casopitant. All three drugs are used to prevent or treat nausea and vomiting associated with conditions such as chemotherapy, postoperative recovery, or motion sickness. Recognizing that metopimazine, nabilone, and casopitant belong to the same ATC group helps clinicians select appropriate antiemetic therapy based on each agent’s pharmacologic profile and the specific needs of the patient.
